name: Cannon Beach
features: Beach breaks near Haystack Rock, iconic Oregon coast

guide.hazards: Haystack Rock currents, sneaker waves, cold water
guide.crowd: Moderate to heavy in summer, light in winter
guide.description: Cannon Beach is one of Oregon's most iconic surf spots, sitting in the shadow of Haystack Rock. The beach break works on most swells and is forgiving enough for beginners while offering solid peaks for intermediates during bigger swells. The main break is south of Haystack Rock, with additional peaks stretching along the wide beach.

name: Indian Beach
features: Ecola State Park, consistent offshore peaks, sea stacks

guide.hazards: Rocky shoreline, strong currents, sea stacks, difficult access
guide.description: Indian Beach in Ecola State Park offers some of the most consistent surf on Oregon's north coast. Protected by the headland, it picks up swell from multiple directions. The rock-sand bottom creates defined peaks that are more powerful than neighboring beach breaks. The hike in keeps crowds manageable.

name: Short Sands
features: Oswald West State Park, horseshoe bay protected from NW winds

guide.bottom: Sandy beach break in a cove
guide.hazards: Rocks on south side, cold water, 15-min hike in
guide.crowd: Can get very crowded on good days
guide.description: Short Sands (Shorty's) at Oswald West State Park is arguably Oregon's most popular surf break. Nestled in a horseshoe-shaped cove, it's protected from prevailing NW winds and catches plenty of swell. The sandy bottom and mellow waves make it ideal for beginners and longboarders, though it can produce head-high+ waves during winter swells. The 15-minute hike through old-growth forest adds to the magic.

name: Manzanita
features: Beach break with scenic coastal town

guide.hazards: Strong currents, sneaker waves, cold water
guide.description: Manzanita offers a long stretch of exposed beach break that picks up plenty of swell. It's less protected than Short Sands, so wind is more of a factor, but when conditions align it can deliver fun, punchy peaks. The quaint town of Manzanita makes for a great surf trip base.

name: Oceanside
features: Tunnel to secret beach, varied breaks

guide.hazards: Rocky bottom, strong currents at tunnel beach
guide.description: Oceanside is a picturesque small village with a beach break that works best on mid-sized W-NW swells. The main beach faces west and picks up plenty of swell. A tunnel through the cliff leads to a more sheltered beach on the south side. The rock-sand bottom can produce wedgy waves.

name: Pacific City / Cape Kiwanda
features: Sandy-bottom breaks for beginners, point breaks for advanced

guide.hazards: Shore break, Cape Kiwanda cliffs, dory boats launching
guide.description: Pacific City at Cape Kiwanda is one of Oregon's most versatile surf spots. The beach break south of the cape is gentle and great for beginners, while the area closer to the sandstone headland produces steeper, more powerful waves. The dory fleet launches off the beach — give them right of way. The Pelican Pub sits right on the beach for post-surf refreshments.

name: Lincoln City
features: Long stretch of beach, Road's End popular

guide.hazards: Sneaker waves, strong currents, cold water
guide.description: Lincoln City's seven miles of beach offer numerous peaks to choose from. Road's End at the north end is the most popular surf spot, partially protected by the headland. The D River access in the middle of town and the beaches near Taft also produce quality waves. Lincoln City Surf Shop is the local hub for conditions info.

guide.hazards: Extremely dangerous, tow-in only, hold-downs, cold water, rocks
guide.description: Nelscott Reef is Oregon's premier big wave spot, located about half a mile offshore from Lincoln City. It breaks on deep-water reef at 25-40+ feet and is strictly a tow-in affair. The reef was rediscovered in 2003 and has since hosted invitational big wave competitions. Only for experienced big wave surfers with proper safety teams.

name: Otter Rock
features: Partially protected from NW winds, consistent

guide.description: Otter Rock (Devil's Punchbowl) offers a partially protected cove that picks up swell consistently. The cobblestone-sand bottom creates fun peaks that are manageable for beginners on smaller days. The main break is in the cove south of Devil's Punchbowl. Access is easy from the parking lot.

name: South Beach / Newport
features: Gentle beach break, South Beach State Park

guide.hazards: Jetty currents, sneaker waves, cold water
guide.description: South Beach in Newport is a long, exposed beach break south of the Yaquina Bay jetty. The jetty provides some protection and can create sandbars that focus the swell. South Beach State Park offers easy access and parking. Newport's aquarium and bayfront are nearby for non-surfing family members.

name: Florence South Jetty
features: Siuslaw River meets Pacific, gentle break for longboards

guide.hazards: Jetty currents, river mouth currents, sneaker waves
guide.description: Florence South Jetty sits where the Siuslaw River meets the Pacific Ocean. The jetty creates sandbars that produce fun, mellow waves ideal for longboarding. It can handle bigger swells too, when the sandbar is right. The Oregon Dunes stretch south from here. Access is from South Jetty Road.

guide.hazards: Strong currents, rocky areas, cold water, isolated
guide.description: Coos Bay area offers several beach breaks along Bastendorff Beach and nearby shores. Bastendorff Beach, just west of the bay mouth, is the most accessible and consistent. The jetty at the bay entrance can create quality sandbars. This is uncrowded surf territory with a rugged, remote feel.

guide.hazards: Rocks, sea stacks, cold water, isolated
guide.description: Bandon's beach is one of Oregon's most scenic surf spots, with dramatic sea stacks including iconic Face Rock. The beach break between the rocks can produce quality waves, especially on S-SW swells that other north coast spots miss. The town of Bandon (Old Town) has a charming coastal vibe. Bullards Beach State Park is nearby.

guide.bottom: Sandy beach break near river mouth
guide.hazards: River currents, sneaker waves, isolated, cold water
guide.description: Gold Beach sits at the mouth of the Rogue River in southern Oregon. The beach break north of the river mouth picks up both W and SW swells. The river mouth creates shifting sandbars that can produce quality peaks. This is remote, uncrowded surfing — you may have the lineup to yourself.

Brookings is Oregon's southernmost surf spot, just north of the California border. The beach breaks around the Chetco River mouth offer the state's warmest water temperatures due to a unique microclimate. Harris Beach State Park has several accessible breaks. The banana belt climate makes this a relatively pleasant cold-water surf destination.

Rockaway Beach is a family-friendly seven-mile stretch of sand on Oregon's north coast. The gentle beach break is perfect for beginners and longboarders. Multiple access points along the town make it easy to find a peak to yourself. The Twin Rocks offshore provide a scenic backdrop.
